title: Etymology
text: The word platypus comes from Greek platys (flat) and pous (foot).

title: Venom
text: Male platypuses have a spur on their hind foot that delivers a potent venom.

title: Discovery
text: When scientists first saw a specimen, they thought it was a hoax made of different animals sewn together.

title: Reproduction
text: Platypuses are one of only five species of mammals that lay eggs.

title: Defense
text: Male platypuses have venomous spurs on their hind legs.

title: Electroreception
text: Platypuses can detect electric fields generated by their prey using their bills.

title: Offspring
text: A baby platypus is called a puggle.

title: Anatomy
text: Platypuses don't have stomachs. Their esophagus connects directly to their intestines.

title: Navigation
text: Platypuses close their eyes and ears while swimming and rely on their bill to navigate.

title: Symbolism
text: The platypus is the animal emblem of New South Wales, Australia.

title: Cheek Pouches
text: Platypuses can store up to 900 insects in their cheek pouches.

title: Appetite
text: A platypus can consume its own body weight in food in a 24 hour period.

title: Waterproofing
text: Platypuses have two layers of fur that trap air next to their skin, keeping them dry and buoyant for the 12 hours they spend underwater each day searching for food.
